  1. 🐼 Data Manipulation
  2. Data Manipulation with Pandas

🐼 Data Manipulation with Pandas

Merging Tables, GroupBy, Pivot Tables, and String Operations

Author

S.A.M.Perera - AS2022296

🧭 Introduction

Real datasets are rarely one tidy table. They’re split across files, grouped in odd ways, and full of messy text. This guide walks through four everyday pandas tools that fix exactly that, using one simple, running example: a small school store.

The main topics covered are:

  • 🔗 Merge & Join — stitch separate tables together
  • 📊 GroupBy — summarize rows by category
  • 🧩 Pivot Tables — turn long data into a readable grid
  • ✂️ Vectorized String Operations — clean and search text columns
Code 
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

1️⃣ Combining Tables: Merge & Join

Introduction

Imagine a school store. One table lists students and the club they belong to. A second table lists each club’s faculty advisor. They share a column — club — and pd.merge() uses it to line the rows up correctly, the same way a SQL JOIN would.

A One-to-One Match

Every student appears once, and every club appears once. This is the simplest case.

Code 
students = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": ["Amal", "Nadi", "Kavi", "Roshi"],
    "club": ["Chess", "Art", "Drama", "Robotics"]
})

advisors = pd.DataFrame({
    "club":    ["Chess", "Art", "Drama", "Robotics"],
    "advisor": ["Mr. Silva", "Ms. Perera", "Mr. Fonseka", "Ms. Bandara"]
})

pd.merge(students, advisors)
name club advisor
0 Amal Chess Mr. Silva
1 Nadi Art Ms. Perera
2 Kavi Drama Mr. Fonseka
3 Roshi Robotics Ms. Bandara

A Many-to-One Match

Now suppose several students share the same club. pandas repeats the matching advisor for every student in that club — nothing is lost, nothing is duplicated incorrectly.

Code 
students2 = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": ["Amal", "Sajith", "Nadi", "Roshi"],
    "club": ["Chess", "Chess", "Art", "Art"]
})

pd.merge(students2, advisors)
name club advisor
0 Amal Chess Mr. Silva
1 Sajith Chess Mr. Silva
2 Nadi Art Ms. Perera
3 Roshi Art Ms. Perera

A Many-to-Many Match

If a club can have several advisors and several students share that club, every student gets matched with every advisor for that club.

Code 
co_advisors = pd.DataFrame({
    "club": ["Chess", "Chess", "Art", "Drama"],
    "advisor": ["Mr. Silva", "Mrs. Costa", "Ms. Perera", "Mr. Fonseka"]
})

pd.merge(students2, co_advisors)
name club advisor
0 Amal Chess Mr. Silva
1 Amal Chess Mrs. Costa
2 Sajith Chess Mr. Silva
3 Sajith Chess Mrs. Costa
4 Nadi Art Ms. Perera
5 Roshi Art Ms. Perera

Choosing What to Keep: the how Parameter

Sometimes a club has no students yet, or a student’s club isn’t in the advisor list. The how parameter decides what happens to these unmatched rows.

how= value What it keeps
inner (default) Keep only rows that match in both tables
outer Keep every row from both tables; fill gaps with NaN
left Keep every row from the left table
right Keep every row from the right table
Code 
new_club = pd.DataFrame({"club": ["Music"], "advisor": ["Mr. Jay"]})
advisors_plus = pd.concat([advisors, new_club], ignore_index=True)

pd.merge(students, advisors_plus, how="outer")
name club advisor
0 Nadi Art Ms. Perera
1 Amal Chess Mr. Silva
2 Kavi Drama Mr. Fonseka
3 NaN Music Mr. Jay
4 Roshi Robotics Ms. Bandara

When Column Names Don’t Match

If the shared information is stored under different column names, point pandas to each one with left_on and right_on.

Code 
payments = pd.DataFrame({
    "student_name": ["Amal", "Nadi", "Kavi"],
    "amount_due":   [500, 300, 450]
})

pd.merge(students, payments, left_on="name", right_on="student_name")
name club student_name amount_due
0 Amal Chess Amal 500
1 Nadi Art Nadi 300
2 Kavi Drama Kavi 450

Merging on the Index with .join()

.join() is a shortcut for merging on the index instead of a column — handy once both tables share the same index.

Code 
students_idx = students.set_index("name")
payments_idx = payments.set_index("student_name")

students_idx.join(payments_idx)
club amount_due
name
Amal Chess 500.0
Nadi Art 300.0
Kavi Drama 450.0
Roshi Robotics NaN

💡 Tip: Use join() once both DataFrames are indexed the same way (e.g. after set_index()). Otherwise, stick with merge() and specify the key columns directly.

2️⃣ Aggregation & GroupBy

Introduction

GroupBy follows a simple three-step idea called split–apply–combine: split rows by a category, apply a calculation to each group, and combine the results into one tidy table.

Sample Data: Store Sales by Day

Code 
sales = pd.DataFrame({
    "item":     ["Pen", "Pen", "Notebook", "Notebook", "Eraser", "Eraser"],
    "day":      ["Mon", "Tue", "Mon", "Tue", "Mon", "Tue"],
    "quantity": [12, 9, 5, 7, 20, 15],
    "revenue":  [240, 180, 250, 350, 100, 75]
})

sales
item day quantity revenue
0 Pen Mon 12 240
1 Pen Tue 9 180
2 Notebook Mon 5 250
3 Notebook Tue 7 350
4 Eraser Mon 20 100
5 Eraser Tue 15 75

Total Sales per Item

Code 
sales.groupby("item")[["quantity", "revenue"]].sum()
quantity revenue
item
Eraser 35 175
Notebook 12 600
Pen 21 420

Multiple Statistics at Once

Pass a list of function names to .agg() to see several summaries side by side.

Code 
sales.groupby("item")["revenue"].agg(["min", "mean", "max"])
min mean max
item
Eraser 75 87.5 100
Notebook 250 300.0 350
Pen 180 210.0 240

Filtering Entire Groups

filter() keeps or drops whole groups based on a test — for example, only items that brought in more than 300 total revenue.

Code 
sales.groupby("item").filter(
    lambda g: g["revenue"].sum() > 300
)
item day quantity revenue
0 Pen Mon 12 240
1 Pen Tue 9 180
2 Notebook Mon 5 250
3 Notebook Tue 7 350

Transform: Same Shape, Adjusted Values

transform() returns a result the same size as the original data — useful for things like comparing each day’s revenue to its item’s average.

Code 
sales.groupby("item")["revenue"].transform(
    lambda x: x - x.mean()
)
0    30.0
1   -30.0
2   -50.0
3    50.0
4    12.5
5   -12.5
Name: revenue, dtype: float64

Apply: Full Flexibility

apply() is the most flexible GroupBy operation — it can return anything, including a modified version of each group.

Code 
def add_share(group):
    group["pct_of_item_revenue"] = (
        group["revenue"] / group["revenue"].sum() * 100
    )
    return group

sales.groupby("item").apply(add_share)
day quantity revenue pct_of_item_revenue
item
Eraser 4 Mon 20 100 57.142857
5 Tue 15 75 42.857143
Notebook 2 Mon 5 250 41.666667
3 Tue 7 350 58.333333
Pen 0 Mon 12 240 57.142857
1 Tue 9 180 42.857143

Quick Reference

Method What it does
count() Number of non-missing values
sum() / mean() Total or average per group
min() / max() Smallest or largest value per group
std() / var() Spread within each group
first() / last() First or last row in each group
describe() Full summary statistics per group

3️⃣ Pivot Tables

Introduction

A pivot table answers questions like “how does X compare across Y and Z?” in one line — combining grouping, aggregating, and reshaping into a single, spreadsheet-style view.

Sample Data: Store Survey Results

Code 
survey = pd.DataFrame({
    "grade":     ["Grade 9", "Grade 9", "Grade 10", "Grade 10", "Grade 11", "Grade 11"],
    "item":      ["Pen", "Notebook", "Pen", "Notebook", "Pen", "Notebook"],
    "satisfied": [1, 1, 0, 1, 1, 0]
})

survey
grade item satisfied
0 Grade 9 Pen 1
1 Grade 9 Notebook 1
2 Grade 10 Pen 0
3 Grade 10 Notebook 1
4 Grade 11 Pen 1
5 Grade 11 Notebook 0

The GroupBy Approach

Code 
survey.groupby(["grade", "item"])["satisfied"].mean().unstack()
item Notebook Pen
grade
Grade 10 1.0 0.0
Grade 11 0.0 1.0
Grade 9 1.0 1.0

The Pivot Table Approach

The same result, written more concisely. Place grade on the rows and item across the columns to compare satisfaction at a glance.

Code 
survey.pivot_table(
    values="satisfied",
    index="grade",
    columns="item"
)
item Notebook Pen
grade
Grade 10 1.0 0.0
Grade 11 0.0 1.0
Grade 9 1.0 1.0

Adding Totals with margins

Code 
survey.pivot_table(
    values="satisfied",
    index="grade",
    columns="item",
    margins=True
)
item Notebook Pen All
grade
Grade 10 1.000000 0.000000 0.500000
Grade 11 0.000000 1.000000 0.500000
Grade 9 1.000000 1.000000 1.000000
All 0.666667 0.666667 0.666667

Filling in Missing Values

Code 
survey.pivot_table(
    values="satisfied",
    index="grade",
    columns="item",
    fill_value=0
)
item Notebook Pen
grade
Grade 10 1.0 0.0
Grade 11 0.0 1.0
Grade 9 1.0 1.0

Grouping by More Than One Variable

Code 
survey["term"] = ["Term 1", "Term 1", "Term 2", "Term 2", "Term 1", "Term 2"]

survey.pivot_table(
    values="satisfied",
    index=["grade", "term"],
    columns="item"
)
item Notebook Pen
grade term
Grade 10 Term 2 1.0 0.0
Grade 11 Term 1 NaN 1.0
Term 2 0.0 NaN
Grade 9 Term 1 1.0 1.0

💡 Tip: Add fill_value=0 to replace empty cells with 0 instead of NaN, and pass a list to index= or columns= to group by more than one variable at once.

4️⃣ Vectorized String Operations

Introduction

The .str accessor applies a string method to an entire column at once. It also skips over missing values automatically, so a stray None won’t crash the whole operation.

Sample Data: Messy Customer Names

Code 
names = pd.Series(["amal silva", "NADI perera", None, "Kavi RATNAYAKE"])

names.str.title()
0        Amal Silva
1       Nadi Perera
2               NaN
3    Kavi Ratnayake
dtype: str

Basic String Methods

Code 
names.str.lower()
0        amal silva
1       nadi perera
2               NaN
3    kavi ratnayake
dtype: str
Code 
names.str.upper()
0        AMAL SILVA
1       NADI PERERA
2               NaN
3    KAVI RATNAYAKE
dtype: str
Code 
names.str.len()
0    10.0
1    11.0
2     NaN
3    14.0
dtype: float64
Code 
pd.Series([" Amal ", " Nadi "]).str.strip()
0    Amal
1    Nadi
dtype: str

Searching with contains()

Find every entry that includes a pattern — useful for filtering rows by a keyword.

Code 
orders = pd.Series(["2 pens, 1 ruler", "3 notebooks", "1 eraser, 2 pens"])

orders.str.contains("pen", case=False)
0     True
1    False
2     True
dtype: bool

Replacing Text

Code 
orders.str.replace("pen", "PEN", case=False, regex=False)
0     2 PENs, 1 ruler
1         3 notebooks
2    1 eraser, 2 PENs
dtype: str

Extracting Parts of Text with Regex

Code 
contacts = pd.Series(["Amal-0771234567", "Nadi-0719876543"])

contacts.str.extract(r"(\w+)-(\d+)")
0 1
0 Amal 0771234567
1 Nadi 0719876543

Splitting Strings

Code 
contacts.str.split("-", expand=True)
0 1
0 Amal 0771234567
1 Nadi 0719876543

One-Hot Encoding with get_dummies()

Code 
pd.Series(["pens|rulers", "notebooks", "pens|erasers"]).str.get_dummies("|")
erasers notebooks pens rulers
0 0 0 1 1
1 0 1 0 0
2 1 0 1 0

Putting It Together: Tagging Inventory

Combine contains() with a list of keywords to flag which products appear in each order — a common first step before grouping or filtering further.

Code 
order_text = pd.Series([
    "pens and rulers",
    "notebooks only",
    "pens, erasers, rulers"
])

items = ["pens", "rulers", "notebooks", "erasers"]

tags = pd.DataFrame({
    item: order_text.str.contains(item)
    for item in items
})

tags
pens rulers notebooks erasers
0 True True False False
1 False False True False
2 True True False True

Finding Orders with Specific Items

Code 
mask = tags["pens"] & tags["rulers"]

order_text[mask]
0          pens and rulers
2    pens, erasers, rulers
dtype: str

Quick Reference

Method What it does
.str.lower() / .str.upper() Change case of every entry
.str.strip() Remove leading/trailing whitespace
.str.contains() Test for a pattern, returns True/False
.str.replace() Swap matching text for something else
.str.extract() Pull out parts of text using a pattern
.str.split() Break text into pieces around a separator
.str.get_dummies() Turn tags into one column per category

🏆 Key Takeaways

🔗 Merge & Join

  • Combines tables using a shared column — works just like a SQL join.
  • Supports one-to-one, many-to-one, and many-to-many matches.
  • how= (inner, outer, left, right) controls which unmatched rows survive.
  • Use left_on / right_on when the shared column has different names in each table.

📊 GroupBy

  • Implements the split–apply–combine paradigm.
  • agg() runs several statistics at once; filter() drops entire groups that fail a test.
  • transform() keeps the original shape; apply() offers full flexibility.

🧩 Pivot Tables

  • A faster, more readable alternative to groupby() + unstack().
  • margins=True adds row/column totals; fill_value=0 cleans up empty cells.
  • Supports grouping by multiple variables at once.

✂️ String Operations

  • The .str accessor applies text operations across a whole column, skipping missing values.
  • contains(), split(), and extract() turn messy text into structured, analyzable columns.
  • get_dummies() converts categorical text tags into indicator columns.
